title: McKenzie insists she didn't misuse taxpayer money to attend son's wedding
text:
Coalition Senator Bridget McKenzie has defended billing taxpayers for a trip that included a family wedding, and signalled she won't pay the money back.

She spoke with Today about the February 2023 four-day trip to Tasmania which costed taxpayers ﻿$853.52, and included her attending the wedding of her son.

It was first reported in ﻿ The Age.

READ MORE: Senate hearing derailed by fiery stoush between McKenzie and Labor MP

Coalition front bencher told Today she paid her own way when attending her son's wedding in Tasmania three years ago. (Nine)

McKenzie ﻿has insisted her travel was genuine political business, saying taxpayers did fork out for any expenses over the wedding.

"Taxpayer funds should never be used for private business, nor were they in this case," she told Today.

"When we're undertaking whether it be community engagement, media engagement, stakeholder meetings, you know, assisting colleagues, there are arrangements in place to assist us to do that work that is right and appropriate, and it is not right and appropriate to bill the taxpayer, as you say, for personal activities. And I did not do that.

"So when I was on personal business, I absolutely paid my own way."

When asked if she stood by her decision, she replied: "Yep".

McKenzie maintained she had behaved correctly when fellow guest James Willis from 2GB suggested the Coalition front bencher should pay the money back.

The episode has dogged her over past days, with a leading Liberal MP and shadow minister saying the optics didn't appear good.

Coalition treasury spokesman Tim Wilson said the trip to Tasmania was technically within parliamentary guidelines, but it failed the "pub test".

title: Man seemingly caught asleep while driving on major motorway
text:
A Tesla driver has been caught seemingly asleep behind the wheel while driving on one of Sydney's busiest motorways.

The video, shared on Instagram, appears to show the man with his eyes closed and leaning back in his seat. His hands are not on the wheel of his white Tesla.

A geolocation tag on the post suggests the man is driving on the M2 Motorway, a major roadway that travels from Sydney's north-west towards the North Shore and the Sydney Harbour Bridge.

The driver acknowledged the person videoing him. (Instagram/@luke.bandiera)

The post was captioned with "arvo snooze", in a reference to the driver appearing to be asleep despite being in the driver's seat of the car.

After a few seconds, the man, who is wearing a seatbelt, opens his eyes and puts his hands on the steering wheel, waving a hand towards the person filming him, potentially looking to signal he is alert and awake.

The man was most likely using Tesla's Autopilot mode, which can allow the driver to use cruise control to maintain the speed of the car while also holding a safe distance between other cars on the road.

Technically speaking, drivers using Tesla's autopilot mode are supposed to be "fully attentive". (Instagram/@luke.bandiera)

It also offers an autosteer setting, where the car maintains a set speed if there is no car in front, and automatically detects lane markings, road edges and the presence of other objects on the road to keep the car in its lane.

Tesla's official website states users must still be fully attentive while engaged in Autopilot mode.

It says that if the car detects the driver not paying enough attention, they will be locked out of autopilot mode.

title: Australia's richest people - and how they amassed their fortunes - revealed
text:
As ordinary Australians have battled cost-of-living pressures over the past year, some of the country's richest people have grown their substantial fortunes.

The Australian Financial Review has revealed its 2026 Rich List, and the country's 200 largest fortunes have collectively leapt by $39 billion to a total of $707 billion.

﻿When the rich list was first published in the Business Review Weekly magazine in 1983, the total worth of Australia's wealthiest people was $4.6 billion.

Gina Rinehart has topped the Rich List for the seventh consecutive year. (Getty)

Mining magnate Gina Rinehart has topped the rich list for the seventh consecutive year, with an estimated wealth of $39 billion, up $900 million from last year.

Meriton founder Harry Triguboff ﻿is number two on the list.

The property developer is worth an estimated $32.29 billion, up from $29.65 billion.

Visy executive chairman Anthony Pratt and his family are ranked third on the list.

Their fortune is worth an estimated $25.19 billion, which they amassed by running Australia's biggest packaging and recycling company.

Former Glencore chief executive Ivan Glasenberg saw his wealth explode from $13 billion last year to $22.38 billion this year.

Meriton founder Harry Triguboff has amassed his fortune through property. (Janie Barrett)

﻿Out of the top 178 rankings of this year's Rich 200 list﻿, just less than one in nine people carried estimated fortunes of $1 billion and above.

While total wealth is up, there have been some falls in the technology sector, as a result of the billions of dollars wiped off the value of listed software-as-a-service companies.﻿

Mike Cannon-Brookes went from 13th on the Rich List to 22nd after his estimated fortune dropped from $12.18 billion last year to $6.71 billion this year.﻿

His Atlassian co-founder Scott Farquhar and wife Kim Jackson, who leads Skip Capital, have dropped from fourth to 13th after the couple saw their wealth plummet from $21.4 billion to $11.7 billion.

Scott Farquhar and Mike Cannon Brookes both dropped down the list. (Dominic Lorrimer)

WiseTech founder Richard White went from 15th to 17th after his wealth dropped by nearly $2 billion to $8.8 billion.

Marriage breakdowns continue to reshape the top Rich List rankings, with miner Andrew Forrest falling from Australia's second-richest person in 2023 to eighth this year, with a fortune worth $15.93 billion.

He was overtaken by his former wife, Nicola, who is ranked seventh at $17.32 billion.

Some Rich Listers have amassed their fortunes from artificial intelligence and data centres, including Swipejobs founder Katrina Leslie (worth $2.5 billion), Firmus co-founder Oliver Curtis (worth $1.25 billion) and Grafana Labs co-founder Anthony Woods (worth $881 million).

Andrew Forrest was overtaken by his former wife, Nicola, who is ranked seventh at $17.32 billion. (Kate Geraghty/ The Sydney Morning Herald)

Australian Financial Review Rich List editor Hannah Tattersall said behind each Rich Lister was a story about they built their wealth and where their ideas came from.

"Mining, property and tech always dominate the Rich List but this year's list shows there's money to be made in waste management, in chicken restaurants, in hoodies and of course AI," Tattersall said.

The Financial Review Rich List 2026's top 10

1. Mining magnate and businesswoman Gina Rinehart – $39.01 billion – Mining

2. Meriton founder and managing director Harry Triguboff - $32.29 billion - Property

3. Visy Industries executive chairman Anthony Pratt and family – $25.19 billion – Manufacturing

4. Former Glencore CEO Ivan Glasenberg – $22.38 billion – Mining

5. Mining magnate Clive Palmer – $19.56 billion – Mining

6. Canva founders Melanie Perkins and Cliff Obrecht – $17.56 billion – Technology

7. Philanthropist Nicola Forrest - $17.32 billion - Mining

8. Miner Andrew Forrest - $15.93 billion – Mining

9. Stonepeak founder Michael Dorrell - $13.82 billion - Investment

10. Chemist Warehouse founders Mario, Marcello & Adrian Verrocchi - $12.76 billion - Retail

Where Australia's Rich Listers are based﻿

title: Monster catch worth up to $70,000 but Aussie trio will never see a cent
text:
Three brothers have hooked a monster tuna weighing 107.5 kilograms off the coast of south-west Victoria .



Peter, Michael and Andreas Salloum were fishing with their cousin in Apollo Bay on the state's Surf Coast this week when they came across the southern bluefin.

It took two hours for the four to wrangle the massive fish.

The four spent two hours wrangling the massive fish. (9News)

"We were all in just pure shock," Michael told 9News.

"Even the charter captain we had, he was in disbelief as well."﻿

Southern bluefin tuna are among the world's most prized fish, fetching up to $683 per kilogram, however recreational fishers in Australia can't sell their catch.

Three brothers have hooked a monster tuna weighing 107.5 kilos off the coast of south-west Victoria. (9News)

The brothers have shared their monster trophy among family and friends.

"Some of it is in our freezer, some of it is in our bellies and some of it has been eaten by our friends and family," Michael said.

"Definitely something we'll remember, a hundred kilos is not something you catch every day," Peter said.

title: Hopes inquest into Hillcrest jumping castle tragedy will begin in September
text:
A coronial inquest into the deaths of six children at a Tasmanian primary school could begin in September, nearly five years after the tragedy occurred.

At a case management conference at the Hobart Magistrates Court this morning, details of the inquest's proposed scope and witness list were outlined.

The court heard coroner Olivia McTaggart was working toward an initial hearing in the second half of September at the Paranaple Centre in Devonport.

In December 2021, six children died, and three others were injured, when a freak wind lifted an inflatable jumping castle into the air during end-of-year celebrations at Hillcrest Primary School in Devonport.

Devonport council approves Hillcrest jumping castle memorial A proposed memorial for the child victims of the Hillcrest jumping castle tragedy has been unanimously approved by the Devonport City Council.

Ms McTaggart told the court she would like the inquest to begin in September, "particularly for the sake of the families".

The inquest was previously postponed while a criminal case into the tragedy was underway.

It has since resumed, after Rosemary Gamble, the owner of the business that supplied the jumping castle, was found not guilty of failing in her work health and safety duty in June last year.

Details of the inquest's scope and witness list are yet to be finalised, but Ms McTaggart said a decision on a start date must be made by the next case management conference at the end of July.

Olivia McTaggart says she wants the inquest to begin by late February "for the sake of the families". (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Families call for staff to appear

During today's proceedings, the court heard various submissions to amend and broaden both the scope of the inquest and the witness list.

Maree Norton SC, counsel assisting the coroner, said some families want school staff to be added to the witness list.

This includes the school's principal at the time and the teacher who organised the end-of-school celebrations.

This request was opposed by the state, on the grounds that they have already provided evidence in the form of affidavits and responses to WorkSafe.

The court heard the state believed there was "nothing to be gained" from calling those additional witnesses, who are also traumatised by the events.

The court heard families of the victims wanted school staff, including the former principal, to appear in the inquest. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

The state said a senior official from the Department of Education would appear.

Ms Norton asked for this issue to be deferred, saying the coroner would be in a better position to determine who would need to be called as a witness when the scope was finalised.

She acknowledged this would leave those involved in a "somewhat uncomfortable state of limbo".

No plans to reinflate jumping castle

Ms Norton told the court the proposed scope of the inquest would include whether there were deficits or inadequacies by the Department of Education, which contributed to the tragedy.

Ms Norton said this would include the selection of Ms Gamble's company Taz-Zorb to supply the jumping castle, what risk assessments were undertaken, and weather monitoring on the day of the event.

The court also heard no applications had been made to reinflate the jumping castle during the inquest.

Chris Dockray, appearing on behalf of Ms Gamble, said his client may look to include an inquiry into potential deficits or inadequacies by the jumping castle's manufacturer, East Inflatables, which may have contributed to the tragedy.

He also told the court his clients may propose an inquiry into potential deficits or inadequacies in the regulation or inspection of inflatable devices by WorkSafe Tasmania.

Taz-Zorb owner Rosemary Anne Gamble was found not guilty of a health and safety charge following the incident. (ABC News: Morgan Timms)

Hopes inquest will be held in September

Ms McTaggart told the court all parties would have seven days to file any application to further amend the inquest's scope, and a further seven days to reply to those submissions.

She said the court should be in a position by the next case management conference to secure hearing dates in the second half of September, pending the finalisation of the inquest's scope.

She told the court the inquest would likely be held in blocks, due to the availability of the Paranaple Centre in Devonport.

The next case management conference will be held at the Hobart Magistrates Court on July 24.

title: Greens signal willingness to help Labor push capital gains changes through in weeks
text:
The Greens appear willing to cooperate with Labor's timeline to legislate its capital gains tax and negative gearing changes within weeks, indicating they are unlikely to support a Coalition push for a months-long inquiry into the contentious proposal.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers introduced the first round of legislation to the lower house today, sketching out the framework for an inflation-based CGT discount and curbs to negative gearing for property investors.

The bill also contains Labor's $250 tax cut for wage earners and its $1,000 universal deduction for work-related expenses, but does not include the thorny details of the capital gains treatment of start-ups and small businesses.

Those have been deferred until later, with the treasurer confirming today he was consulting with "small and start-up businesses", which have been the focal point of a fierce backlash from a broad array of business groups.

Mr Chalmers said the laws would overturn "more than two decades of a distorted tax system" that had fuelled property price growth. "There's no point having a ladder if the first few rungs are missing … We are not just acknowledging this, we are acting to fix it".

Greens confident 'scrutiny' possible by late June

The Greens have long argued that negative gearing and the CGT discount are unfair and should be abolished, but have not said whether they will vote for Labor's bill, which they believe does not go far enough.

Their hesitation had fuelled speculation that they might team up with the Coalition, which wants a months-long inquiry to drum up further opposition.

Loading

But in a statement, party leader Larissa Waters and economic justice spokesperson Nick McKim indicated that a shorter-term Senate inquiry, which will conclude on June 22, would be enough to give the bill "the scrutiny it deserves".

"We will use this inquiry to examine how and why Labor decided to leave in place the vast majority of tax handouts for the ultra-wealthy," Senator McKim said.

What Labor's tax changes really mean for you Labor's capital gains tax changes have set off a firestorm of debate about the implications for small businesses, start-ups, share owners and plenty more. Here's who will really pay more.

Despite that criticism, a Greens source said the party was not inclined to help the Coalition drag out the legislative process and intended to be constructive. The Greens' main objection is Labor's decision to carve out existing investments and gains.

An inquiry completed by June 22 would give the government enough time for the legislation to pass before parliament rises for the lengthy winter break on July 2.

The inquiry was triggered automatically by a motion Labor moved several weeks ago, in what appears to have been a strategy to allow the committee process to proceed as quickly as possible, given that the Senate is not sitting this fortnight.

Loading

A Coalition spokesperson suggested the June 22 inquiry was an accident on Labor's part, saying it was "making up their budget and higher taxes as they go along", but Labor denied this.

Independent Allegra Spender moved a motion in the lower house for an inquiry ending in late July, but this was defeated.

Labor can achieve a majority in the Senate with the Greens' support, meaning the Coalition could not succeed in its bid for a longer inquiry without the minor party.

Small business concessions may be expanded

Start-up founders have complained that the changes would disadvantage them relative to other businesses because of the way the new tax regime would treat those who start from businesses scratch.

Whereas the existing discount, a flat 50 per cent, taxes capital gains of the same size in the same way, the proposed inflation-based system varies depending on the size of the "cost base" of the investment.

Housing investors are increasingly older and wealthier, RBA research shows Reserve Bank research shows property investment has become skewed towards higher-income households and is dominated by baby boomers.

The cost base is what the discount is applied to. In the case of a property, it will generally be the purchase price (with some adjustments). That price is adjusted for any inflation that occurs while the property is held, and then only the "real" gain is taxed.

But for a start-up founder selling a business they started on a laptop, the base would be near zero, meaning effectively that there is nothing to inflate, and that the discount is smaller. The budget papers nodded to this problem but offered no solution.

While the government initially indicated it would devise a workaround for tech start-ups, which might be expected to have the lowest cost bases, it has been met with petitions to broaden this to all start-ups and other businesses.

Small businesses with turnover under $2 million, the vast majority, already get an additional 50 per cent discount in some cases and can access a variety of other concessions which Labor does not propose to change.

But small-business lobby COSBOA has pushed for a larger threshold, something the government appears poised to consider.

Other business groups and some crossbenchers have called for a broader rethink, with capital gains changes confined to investment properties alone and not to other asset classes.

Mr Chalmers has argued this would create undesirable distortions and that consistent treatment across asset classes is preferable. The government argues its changes will bring the tax treatment of investment closer to that of wages, though not exactly aligned.

title: 'Really the wrong thing': Solomon refuses to consider full-time Dons gig
text:
Interim Essendon coach Dean Solomon says he currently has no interest pursuing the full-time role after replacing Brad Scott.

Solomon will lead Essendon this weekend against West Coast after Scott was sacked on Tuesday despite being contracted through the 2027 season.

Having previously served on Essendon's board, Solomon joined Scott's coaching staff at the end of last year after Daniel Giansiracusa's departure to Hawthorn left a vacancy. He denied reports suggesting he and Scott had a rift.

Scott's sacking gives Essendon another chance to step out of Hird shadow Essendon finds itself at a clear demarcation point after sacking Brad Scott as the shadow of James Hird once again looms large over the troubled club.

"I won't even consider [taking the job full-time]. I think it's really the wrong thing to do for me to entertain any thoughts around that," Solomon told reporters on Thursday morning.

"I've got to knuckle down — my last 48 hours has been chaos. I actually don't have the capacity to take that top spot on at the moment.

"It won't be in-season … I might struggle, or dislike the role, so who knows … I won't consider it in-season."

Solomon's comments come just days after his former teammate James Hird spectacularly declared his desire to coach Essendon again, 11 years after his previous stint.

Dean Solomon denies rumours of a rift between him and departed Essendon coach Brad Scott (pictured). (Getty Images: Daniel Pockett)

"I love the fact that he feels he's in a position to want to coach again," he said of Hird's interest in returning to Essendon.

"On a personal level I'm glad that's the case. Whether that happens or not, I've got no… I'm not part of any of those discussions."

Hird's public interest in the role has caused a stir in the AFL world, with potential candidates Adam Simpson and Ken Hinkley both suggesting they are not interested in the Essendon job due to Hird's presence.

Simpson was particularly scathing when discussing the impact of Hird's public candidacy on Essendon's coach hunt.

West Coast's 2018 premiership coach, Adam Simpson, refuses to be involved in a process that involves Hird. (AAP Image: Joe Castro)

"What are they doing? It will be a one-man process at the moment. I can't see anyone going for this job," he told Fox Footy's AFL360 on Wednesday night.

"Going for a job where you know there's an inside running happening — I went for the Brisbane job [and Justin] Leppitsch got it.

"I thought I'll back myself in … but I didn't realise Leigh Matthews was his [Leppitsch's] mentor. I think Leigh might have been on the panel.

"I don't say it [the Brisbane process] was a boat race but, when I left, it I was like, 'OK, how did that feel?'

John Longmire is the highest-profile candidate on the coaching market. (Getty Images: AFL Photos/Robert Cianflone)

"I really enjoyed the experience, but looking at it now it would be difficult to run for that [Essendon] position until they get that sorted. I think Ken [Hinkley] nailed it today."

Former Sydney coach John Longmire is also a contender for the Essendon job, as well as Carlton and Tasmania's vacancies, and is arguably the most high-profile candidate.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Unlike Hinkley and Simpson, Longmire has not ruled himself out of being involved in Essendon's process.

Amid speculation that Essendon president Andrew Welsh, also an ex-player at the club, would solely hunt an ex-player to be the next Bombers coach, Solomon disputed that narrative.

"You don't have to be an Essendon person to coach Essendon," he said.

"There's so many examples of people who played at the club who coached the club, there's people who haven't played at the club…

"There's a lot of success stories. You don't have to be an Essendon person to coach this club."

title: Shopping habits to help reduce your grocery spend
text:
How we shop for groceries can become "habitual", which might seem beneficial, but experts say it can be unhelpful when it comes to saving money.

"It's effortful to change habits," says Paul Harrison, head of consumer behaviour at Deakin University in Melbourne/Naarm.

"When you're trying to save money, you do have to change the way that you eat and buy things … at least for a time."

We asked experts and families for their advice on small changes that can make a difference to your grocery bill.

Use what you already have

Dr Harrison says, on average, Australian households waste $2,500 to $3,000 a year on food that they throw away.

"Which is a lot if we're trying to save money."

Before mother of two Cassie Head does her grocery shopping, she routinely checks everything that's left in her family's pantry and fridge.

"One thing that I'm really focusing on is trying to reduce food waste," says the content creator and former financial planner, based on the Sunshine Coast/Kabi Kabi lands.

" I'm doing a stocktake of my pantry, fridge and freezer and seeing what I already have, then making a meal plan around that. "

Experts say a list is important, so you're not tempted to buy other products. (ABC News: Maani Truu)

Choice supermarket researcher Marg Rafferty says "shopping from your pantry and fridge" first is an ideal approach to reduce waste and save.

"Recently, my family had a whole lot of Greek yoghurt we hadn't used.

"We turned it into a dressing and did a dish around that product because it was there."

Make a list and stick to it

Once you know what food you have already, you can meal plan around that and make a list of what you need.

Cassie makes sure to include breakfasts, lunches and school snacks in her planning.

How this couple perfected their meal-prep routine Linh Dao and David Tran say three hours of cooking every Sunday helps their whole week run smoothly.

"[I'm] really trying to [minimise] how many times I'm going to the shops."

She also says she buys ingredients that can be used across multiple meals.

"Things like mince, frozen vegetables and legumes, which can make a meal stretch further."

Dr Harrison says whether you're grocery shopping online or in-store, a list is important, so you're not tempted to buy other products.

He recommends families do the planning together, so one member of the household doesn't feel like they're carrying the burden on their own.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

"When everyone has a say in what goes on the list, they're more likely to respect the plan and less likely to make impulse purchases that blow the budget," he says.

"Research in behavioural science suggests that people are far more likely to follow through on decisions that were developed collectively within a strong family unit."

Make fewer visits to the supermarket

Dr Harrison says supermarkets want us coming in more frequently "because they know you'll buy extra products".

"Ultimately, if you're trying to save money, shop less frequently," he says.

" What we've learnt from research is that most supermarkets actually make their best profits in short trips. "

"The entire supermarket experience," adds Ms Rafferty, "from the size of your trolley to the colour of the signage, the overall layout, it's all designed to get you to spend".

Know what your budget is

It sounds simple, but Dr Harrison says most people "don't really know how much they're spending each week".

"Before you leave the house or open an app, have a budget," he says.

"It shouldn't be a guestimate; you should calculate it.

Budgeting as a single mum Budgeting is not just about restricting your spending. It's a tool to help you better manage your money and achieve your financial goals, writes Nataasha Torzsa.

He recommends looking at how much you spend over a period of six weeks and then making adjustments.

Cassie says she likes to keep herself accountable for what is being spent.

"At the end of each month, I go through my bank statement, and I add up every transaction, which can be horrifying," she says.

"But I'm making sure that I know exactly what I am spending and what's a realistic budget."

Swap usual products for cheaper options

Leanne Neiland, from Brisbane/Meanjin, says she has halved her family of four's weekly grocery spend to $200, "by looking at ways to save and being creative".

She tried these shopping habits to reduce her bill:

Using cheaper meats such as mince and chicken wings

Buying heavily marked-down and almost-expired products

Buying lentils and beans to bulk out meals

Using more vegetables and less meat

Eating what's in season

Extending meals to cover two nights, not one

Swapping brand-name products for cheaper alternatives

Using food waste apps that sell "surprise bags" of surplus food

Leanne Neiland has been working to keep the family's weekly grocery spend to $200 or less. (Supplied: Leanne Neiland)

"Eating more whole foods, you're ultimately going to eat less too," Ms Neiland says.

"I have noticed less waste in our garbage bin."

Cassie says marked-down items at supermarkets are usually time-stamped on the corner of the sticker.

"My local store usually does its first markdown at 9am, then they'll mark it down again around 2pm," she says.

Ms Rafferty says checking the unit price of items is also a good way to see whether it is value for money.

"Carefully compare loose and packaged items," she says.

"A lot of people think it's cheaper to buy loose, but we found that pre-packaged was cheaper 35 per cent of the time.

"So, there's no hard and fast rule, and you do need to pay attention to the unit price."

'Struggling isn't failure'

Dr Harrison says if you are finding it difficult to save money on groceries, you are not alone.

"Sometimes you're already making really good decisions, and it's just a struggle," he says.

Experts on the best options if you're short on money If you’ve got an unexpected bill to pay or large expense and you don’t have the money to cover it, here’s what you can do.

"Just sharing the psychological load with others, I think, is always a good thing.

" Like actually saying, 'I'm really struggling', because you'll be surprised how many people say it too. "

He recommends not giving up all the little things in your weekly shop that bring you happiness.

"If you go, 'I really like this brand of chocolate, this is what I'm going to give myself', then do it," he says.

"Sometimes you want to give yourself something that actually makes you happy.

"I think we've got to somehow, in amongst all of this, live a good life at the same time."

title: $30k fine for man who distributed thousands of pamphlets targeting MP
text:
A man has been fined $30,000 over the distribution of tens of thousands of unauthorised, anonymous pamphlets targeting independent MP Allegra Spender in the lead up to last year's federal election.

Jarrod Davis is also required to pay a $15,000 contribution to the Australian Electoral Commission's (AEC) legal costs after it took him to court over the pamphlets, which failed to include an authorisation and his name and address as required for political communications.

According to a statement of facts cited in Federal Court, Mr Davis admitted that in April last year, he organised for about 47,000 copies of the material to be distributed to voters in the seat of Wentworth, where he was also a constituent.

The finance industry executive paid about $17,500 to a company to produce and distribute the pamphlet, which was 16 pages long and included the title "Allegra Spender exposed".

Mr Davis previously told the ABC the incident was "not the biggest deal in the world". (Supplied)

It opposed Ms Spender's re-election, accused her of regularly misleading the electorate, and attempted to link her to the Labor government.

The bottom of each page read, "Produced by the people of Wentworth for the people of Wentworth."

Court orders on the pecuniary penalty and costs were sought as part of an agreed position between the parties.

MP re-elected despite pamphets

Justice Melissa Perry was on Thursday told Mr Davis was acting in a personal capacity and admitted to a single contravention of the Commonwealth Electoral Act.

He could have faced a penalty of up to $39,600 for the breach.

The judge accepted a submission that the fact Ms Spender was ultimately re-elected did not mean there was no loss or damage caused.

Justice Perry noted the parties' joint position that the potential harm "lies in the fact that readers of the pamphlet were denied the ability to properly assess the information contained in the pamphlet by reference to its author".

The leaflet criticised Ms Spender's responses to antisemitic attacks. (Supplied)

She also took into account that Mr Davis has cooperated with the AEC from "the earliest opportunity".

He instructed that there be no more distribution of the pamphlet once the AEC alerted the marketing company to the issue, and said it had been "printed without his details by reason of an oversight".

"I am also satisfied Mr Davis has demonstrated contrition by acknowledging he should have included the required particulars on the pamphlet but failed to do so, and through the acts of cooperation," Justice Perry said.

Mr Davis has previously told ABC NEWS Verify that the incident was "not the biggest deal in the world".

He said the AEC was "well aware" it was an error to not include an authorisation, and previously criticised the commission for bringing the case in the Federal Court rather than just allowing him to pay a fine.

Ms Spender previously told ABC NEWS Verify preventing the distribution of "anonymous misinformation that attempts to influence voters" is vital for democracy.

title: Housing investors are increasingly older and wealthier, Reserve Bank research shows
text:
The share of housing investors aged over 60 has surged in Australia over the past 20 years, while the share of younger investors has retreated, new Reserve Bank research shows.

Housing investment has also become more skewed towards higher-income households, and the share of multiple property owners has increased, it shows.

Why Chalmers reined in the property tax lurks One of the more striking elements from the now well-entrenched program of pre-budget leaks is the lack of outrage from the population or even the opposition.

RBA economist Alexandra Michielsen used new data covering the full population of housing investors in Australia that provided a "materially richer" view of housing investors than had previously been possible.

Her analysis showed that, between 1999/2000 and 2022/23, the share of housing investors aged over 60 surged from 12 to 28 per cent, and the share of housing investors aged 50-59 lifted slightly from 24 to 25 per cent.

But for younger age cohorts it retreated.

Over that 20-year period, the share of housing investors under 30 years of age fell from 9 to 3 per cent.

For 30–39 year olds, it dropped from 26 to 18 per cent. For 40-49 year olds, it fell from 29 to 25 per cent.

Overall, the median age of housing investors in Australia increased from 45 to 51 years over the past 20 years.

Ms Michielsen said the shift in the age of investors reflected both broader population aging and an increasing incidence of property ownership among older cohorts.

Housing investors tend to be older and higher-income earners

The research has been published in the RBA's latest Bulletin, in a chapter titled: Insights from New Data on Australian Housing Investors.

The key input for the analysis was newly available rental property schedule data, which covered individuals who lodged a tax return reporting rental property-related income and expenses.

The data captured the whole population of tax-complying individual housing investors in Australia.

Just one rental in Australia affordable for JobSeekers like Jess Anglicare data shows the private rental market effectively does not exist for poorer Australians.

"This article uses newly available micro-level data to deepen our understanding of housing investor characteristics and leverage positions," the paper says.

"By moving beyond previously available semi-aggregate and survey measures, this comprehensive administrative dataset provide new insights into the implications of investor activity for financial stability."

The research showed that housing investment had become more skewed towards higher-income households over the past 20 years.

It said several factors explained the large share of high-income housing investors in Australia.

It said rising property prices had lifted entry costs, making it harder for lower and middle-income households to purchase property.

Tax concessions have also played a role, it said.

"Higher income investors have greater borrowing capacity and have benefited more from tax concessions such as negative gearing and capital gains discounts," it said.

Share of investors owning multiple properties increases

The research also showed that the share of investors who owned multiple properties has increased in the past 20 years, while the share of investors owning a single investment property had declined.

Victim-survivors in 'diabolical' situation amid rental crisis As Brisbane's rental market hits record high prices and historic low vacancy rates, advocates are warning some domestic violence victims are having to choose between staying in an unsafe home and facing homelessness.

Between 1999/2000 and 2022/23, the share of housing investors who owned just one property fell from about 80 per cent to 70 per cent.

The share of investors who owned two properties increased from about 15 to 20 per cent, and the share of investors who owned three or more properties increased from about 5 to 10 per cent.

Overall, the share of investors owning more than one property increased by 7 percentage points over the past 20 years.

Those with multiple investment properties own about half of all investment properties.

As at 2022/23, there were 2.3 million individual housing investors, equivalent to about 10 per cent of the working-age population.

Investment properties accounted for about 20 per cent of the dwelling stock.

Government introduces housing bill to parliament

The RBA paper was published on the same day the Albanese government introduced its first tranche of budget legislation to parliament.

The government's bill included controversial changes to the taxation of housing, including the capital gains tax discount and negative gearing, which were revealed in its budget two weeks ago.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers told parliament when introducing the bill today that his proposed reforms to the taxation of housing would make it easier for people to buy their first home.

Will property tax changes make housing cheaper for first home buyers? There's no doubt that taking away negative gearing on existing properties will even the playing field but they aren't a cure all.

"After more than two decades of a distorted tax system, property prices have far outstripped wage growth," he said.

"Following policy mistakes made a quarter of a century ago, home ownership has been pushed further and further out of reach, especially for young Australians.

"This bill limits negative gearing on properties purchased after budget night to new builds and returns the capital gains tax to its original intent.

"Our reforms will mean 75,000 more home owners entering the housing market over the next decade, reversing a decade of decline in home ownership rates."

Opposition leader Angus Taylor has vowed to repeal the government's housing reforms if he becomes prime minister.

"Labor's negative gearing changes will hand over housing investment to multinationals and foreign pension funds," he said in his budget reply speech.

"Labor's increase to the capital gains tax will discourage the investment Australia needs to grow.

"The Coalition will fight like hell to prevent Labor's toxic taxes from becoming law. But if they do, I commit that a Coalition government I lead will repeal them," he said.

title: 15yo Indian phenom smashes Cummins to set new IPL sixes record
text:
Australian captain Pat Cummins has been left stunned after seeing his Indian Premier League (IPL) side put to the sword by teenage Indian sensation Vaibhav Sooryavanshi.

The 15-year-old's remarkable 97 guided Rajasthan Royals to a 47-run win over Cummins's Sunrisers Hyderabad in the IPL eliminator on Wednesday, local time, keeping alive their hopes of reaching the final.

Chasing 244, Hyderabad fumbled in front of an incisive Rajasthan attack and was bowled out for 196 in 19.2 overs at a sparsely filled New PCA Stadium, ending its campaign on a disappointing note.

Put into bat, Sooryavanshi tore into the Hyderabad attack from the outset, with the teen smashing a 16-ball half-century, and surpassing Chris Gayle's long-standing record for most sixes in a season.

Sooryavanshi has now hit 65 sixes, eclipsing Gayle's 59 in 2012. He is also the leading runscorer for the current IPL season with 680 runs at a dazzling strike rate of 242.85.

Cummins copped the brunt of Sooryavanshi's damage, finishing with figures of 0-64 off four overs, and said the teenager's blitz left him and his fellow bowlers with very few answers.

"Yeah, he played pretty well. [You] don't feel like you have too many options," Cummins said after the match.

Pat Cummins (left) and the rest of Hyderabad's attack had very little impact against Vaibhav Sooryavanshi. (AP Photo: Ashwini Bhatia)

"Obviously, it's a really good pitch, but the margins are so small, you know. You miss your yorker by a little bit, [and] he doesn't tend to miss them. So yeah, fair play."

Opening alongside Yashasvi Jaiswal, Sooryavanshi drove Rajasthan's charge as the pair raced to 80 without loss in the powerplay, leaving the Hyderabad bowlers struggling for control.

Sooryavanshi went on to hammer 97 off 29 balls, including five fours and 12 sixes, but fell short of breaking Gayle's fastest IPL century record of 30 balls.

Sooryavanshi miscued an upper cut to deep third, leaving him visibly dejected after the dismissal as Smaran Ravichandran completed the catch.

"I thought about it [the hundred] after I got out. At that time, I was just focusing on contributing as much as I can," Sooryavanshi said after being named player of the match.

"Hundreds will come, but the goal is to ensure how we win trophies."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Following the teen's dismissal, Dhruv Jurel (50 off 21) and captain Riyan Parag (26 off 12) tried to elevate the innings, but Hyderabad's bowlers soon stemmed the run flow.

They conceded just 36 runs while taking five wickets in the final five overs, though Rajasthan still posted a formidable total.

Ishan Kishan and Travis Head attempted to set the tempo for Hyderabad, but scoreboard pressure told as they slumped to 4-71 in the powerplay, with Jofra Archer leading the charge.

Archer returned figures of 3-58, while Ravindra Jadeja, Nandre Burger and Sushant Mishra took two wickets each.

Rajasthan will play Gujarat Titans in qualifier two on Friday.

Reuters

title: Controversial CIT CEO gives emphatic denial of EV training misconduct allegations
text:
The controversial chief executive of the Canberra Institute of Technology (CIT), Margot McNeill, has given a spirited defence of the institute, categorically rejecting allegations that student mechanics worked on electric vehicles (EVs) from Hertz car hire without the company's knowledge.

Yesterday, Independent MLA Thomas Emerson used parliamentary privilege to make the claims of a whistleblower.

Dr McNeill said the allegations that unqualified students were taking apart hire cars and putting them back together were untrue and "offensive" to staff and students at CIT.

"CIT categorically rejects the allegations that were reported yesterday because they are factually incorrect," Dr McNeill told 666 ABC Canberra.

"Quite honestly, they are offensive to our wonderful staff and students here at CIT."

Dr McNeill also became audibly emotional while talking about the impact the allegations had on staff.

"It was horrific, frankly, last night, I had so many people contacting me very late at night, really upset," she said.

"These are our critical educators working in critical skills shortage areas that have to show up to work the next day."

Hire car company Hertz offers electric vehicles in its collection. (Supplied: Hertz)

Dr McNeill said CIT's Electric Vehicle Centre of Excellence only recruited "skilled professional educators" to deliver its program, and the first tranche of students were "already working in the automotive industry".

"These are not unqualified people," she said.

She also confirmed that an investigation into the issue was launched in January after receiving a complaint, and that the preliminary findings were that only one hire car was used at the institute, and that it was used solely for travel between campuses and demonstration purposes.

"There's no way the vehicles were being taken apart. That's against all our risk profiles. It's against the wonderful teachers we have at CIT," she said.

"That we were putting people at risk — whether that was student or staff — or the unsuspecting public — that's categorically wrong."

Emerson defends airing whistleblower's claims

This morning, Independent MLA Thomas Emerson stood by the whistleblower's complaint, who he has described as a CIT employee who had tried to escalate the issues internally.

"We've seen more evidence than I've been able to table in the assembly, and I can't see why the whistleblower would seek to make this up," Mr Emerson told 666 ABC Canberra.

"They're an employee at CIT who wants for CIT to thrive and to get back to its core business of actually doing things properly."

Mr Emerson said it was "strange" that CIT was so fast to deny the accusations he tabled in the assembly on Wednesday, despite the fact the institute itself confirmed an investigation into the issue was ongoing.

"It seems strange to come to that conclusion already," he said.

"The whistleblower said there is an endemic culture within CIT of silence, of turning a blind eye to poor or unsafe practices and of disdain towards those who raise concerns or question practices."

CIT CEO Margot McNeill is currently under pressure over her actions during her recruitment process. (ABC News)

CEO herself under the microscope

Dr McNeill has recently been under pressure to resign from her CIT role after an ACT Legislative Assembly inquiry found she "actively misled" the institute's board.

Speaking on ABC radio this morning, she said she "absolutely" stood by her previous statements defending herself.

Dr McNeill was appointed CEO in June 2025, but while applying for the role, did not tell the CIT board she was under investigation by TAFE NSW for "serious misconduct".

The controversy sparked the public inquiry, which led to an assembly committee making seven recommendations and 11 findings, including that Dr McNeill should have disclosed the information about the misconduct investigation to the CIT board in a timely manner.

Dr McNeill has repeatedly said she was under a strict obligation not to share the existence of the investigation with CIT.

Today, she refused to make further comments on the details of the allegations despite repeated questioning.

"I would love to talk in great detail about all of that information, but there's two aspects to it," she said.

"One is that the inquiry in the ACT is still underway, and it's also the subject of legal actions with my previous employer in another jurisdiction."

A privileges committee of the ACT Legislative Assembly has been established to investigate whether Dr McNeill gave false or misleading evidence to the original assembly inquiry.

Dr McNeill told the ABC she would appear before that committee.

title: Emergency hay deliveries resume for WA stations after 'generous' donations
text:
Emergency hay deliveries to cattle stations in drought in northern Western Australia have resumed after being put on hold for months due to the fuel crisis.

Students at a private school in Perth are among those who rallied to raise more than $100,000 to send the truckloads of much-needed livestock feed.

A group of Lions Clubs in the state's south also fundraised to ensure the deliveries could occur.

This week, 14 road trains carting various types of fodder embarked on a journey across the state, from farms along the south coast to pastoral stations in the Gascoyne and Murchison.

The drought has been so severe that some stations have been forced to completely de-stock. (ABC Pilbara: Georgia Hargreaves)

Massive stretches of both regions have seen little to no rainfall in recent years, adding financial pressure on pastoralists who have been hand-feeding cattle to keep them alive.

Students rally for boarders' families

Students at Presbyterian Ladies' College in Perth held a series of fundraisers for the cause during National Boarding Week, raising $52,000.

Year 12 boarding student Zara Ryan had to leave school for two weeks to help destock her family's Minnie Creek station, more than 1,000 kilometres north of Perth in the Upper Gascoyne.

Zara Ryan was called home to help muster and destock drought-affected cattle. (Supplied)

"It's such a blessing to have [the school's] support while I'm trying to still do schoolwork, but also trying to help the animals," she said.

"Most of the girls, mostly day girls at school, don't get to see what actually happens around on the farm.

" They've come up to us and asked questions … and now they all understand. "

Faith Ryan is a year 9 boarding student at PLC. (Supplied: PLC)

Weaning calves are to be taken off their mothers and sent to other properties further south, as Minnie Creek has gone nearly four years without rain.

Ms Ryan's younger sister, Faith, a year 9 student, said the drought was heartbreaking.

"It's hard when you're doing it tough, and it's even harder to know who to ask for help," she said.

Relief for pastoralists

While Tropical Cyclone Narelle brought rain to some parts of the Gascoyne and Murchison earlier this year, it did not extend far inland far.

One of the emergency feed loads arrives at Yarrabubba Station in Meekathara. (Suppled: Lauren Lewis)

Yesterday morning, a load of donated feed arrived at Moorarie Station, about 900km north-east of Perth.

Cattle at the station have not seen green feed since 2023.

John and Claire Mahony have continuously hand-fed cattle in paddocks and in yards.

Mr Mahony said the donated feed brings the family respite to the constant expense of buying in more food to keep stock alive.

Moorarie station is in need of rain to grow fodder for livestock and bring the landscape back to life. (Supplied)

"It's such a relief," Mr Mahony said.

"It's good to know that we will have more (hay) as I think we'll be feeding for the rest of the year at least."

He said the family was very grateful for the community effort in fundraising to pay for the feed run, which has not been supported by the state government despite pleas from pastoralists and the Farmers Across Borders charity.

Community efforts widespread

Farmers Across Borders president Sam Starcevich said she was grateful for all donations from several groups.

"It's been overwhelming, and [we're] just so grateful to everyone, it's just brilliant," she said.

Sam Starcevich says it feels good to "finally" be able to send hay up to where it is needed. (ABC Esperance: Emily Smith)

"We've got about 14 trucks headed north, one with oat and hay that was donated from Kojonup, but the rest of the straw from the Esperance region," she said.

" This is just to help ease the burden. "

The ABC has contacted WA Agriculture Minister Jackie Jarvis for comment.

title: Council to demolish in-demand childcare centre for open space
text:
Parents are angry about the upcoming demolition of an in-demand community-based childcare centre to make open space for the growing population in Sydney's north.

Kelly's Place, which neighbours the Crows Nest Sydney Metro station, faces the wrecking ball at the end of the year.

North Sydney Council plans to expand the nearby Hume Street Park into the council-owned land where the 38-year-old childcare centre is located.

The centre's director, Katie Newton, told 702 ABC Sydney the council had promised the centre relocation until November last year.

Kelly's Place has a grassy rooftop accessible to the public. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

"Our community are very, very frustrated and very emotional, but we're trying to stay strong," Ms Newtown said.

Ms Newton said there was a waitlist of 160 children for her centre, which looked after 40 children a day.

She said the demand was due to the centre's high staffing ratios and community rather than a for-profit model.

Katie Newton says there is a lot of demand for community-based child care in the area. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

"It also means that our community, our parents, have a stronger voice in the care and education of their children," Ms Newton said.

"Parents are showing a preference for community-based, not-for-profit organisations.

"Their goal is quality care and education for children, not making a profit for shareholders."

Parents look for similar child care

Crows Nest resident Aoife Gannon, whose son Cillian attends Kelly's Place, said the area's increasing densification would drive more demand for childcare centres.

Aoife Gannon says the area will need more childcare options as density increases. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

"It's actually alarming now that the council is kicking them out and we're actually having a net loss of services," Ms Gannon said.

" I just feel really sad that other parents can't have the great start that Cillian had. "

Dan Djoenaidi said he put his son Oscar in at Kelly's Place because his wife, an early childhood teacher, wanted a community-based childcare centre.

He said he had been putting his son's name down at alternative community-based places, but the nearest might be two suburbs away in Lane Cove.

Dan Djoenaidi wants to find an alternative community-led childcare centre. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

"When we were looking for a childcare centre we were looking for a community-based [centre], it was very difficult to find the right fit," Mr Djoenaidi said.

"We don't want to put him in long daycare so for part of that time we'd have to get a nanny as well to pick him up and look after him."

Shortage of public open space

A spokesperson for North Sydney Council said open space was a priority due to recent NSW government housing reforms and forecast population growth.

"The reality for many people living in the Crows Nest area is that they do not have access to private backyard open space and instead rely heavily on the limited public open space," the spokesperson said.

" Given the scope and nature of the proposed works, retaining the childcare centre on the existing site while redevelopment occurs is not considered feasible. "

However, Ms Newton said Kelly's Place was covered in a publicly accessible grassy rooftop.

Kelly's Place is just across the road from the Crows Nest Sydney Metro station. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

The spokesperson said the council had looked for alternative locations for Kelly's Place since 2021 but there was nowhere with the suitable regulatory and size requirements necessary for a childcare service.

"One of the key challenges facing both council and community organisations in the area is the exceptionally high cost and limited availability of suitable land," the spokesperson said.

Ms Newton said there was a viable site at Banks Street in North Sydney identified in a November 2025 report to councillors, but the council declined to progress the option.

702 ABC Sydney has approached North Sydney Mayor Zoe Baker for comment.

Councillor James Spencely said he tried to move an urgent motion to consider funding an alternative site using developer contributions, but the motion was not deemed urgent enough to be considered.

"The mayor didn't view … six months to build a whole childcare facility as being urgent," Cr Spencely said.

NSW MP for North Shore Felicity Wilson said she wanted to see the place relocated at a minimum.

"We shouldn't have to choose between open space and high-quality child care; we have to be able to do both," Ms Wilson said.

Community-level child care considered high performing

In its analysis of national childcare quality data, the Centre For Future Work found non-profit, community-run childcare centres outperformed their for-profit peers.

The research centre's policy director, Fiona McDonald, said the centres often had lower staff turnover, more well-regarded educational programs and better child health and safety.

"If you've got a community-run centre, you're likely to have a closer, engaged leadership," Dr McDonald said.

"Whereas if you are running at a for-profit centre as part of a large corporation, you're likely to have a single manager."

title: Hugh Marks denies 'threat' to force ABC news director out of role
text:
Managing director Hugh Marks says he did not threaten to terminate ABC news director Justin Stevens, as senators question the director's sudden resignation announcement yesterday.

Mr Marks also confirmed that Mr Stevens's replacement would be formally named tomorrow. It followed reporting that Reuters executive Simon Robinson would take on the role.

Stevens announced his resignation on Wednesday after four years heading ABC's news division, with his final day being that same day.

Shadow Communications Minister Sarah Henderson pressed Marks for an explanation of the sudden resignation in Senate estimates hearings.

Marks, however, declined to comment.

"[It would be] inappropriate for me to go into detail on specific staff matters," Marks said.

"It's a difficult role, the head of news at the ABC. I think after four years and with intense scrutiny … I think Stevens felt it was the right time for him to move on and pursue other careers.

"I've met a number of people over the years who might be opportunities for that role, I expect an announcement will be made imminently, and we will proceed with a candidate who has the potential to lead the ABC into a bright future."

Hugh Marks said the replacement of the ABC's news director provided the organisation an opportunity to refresh and rejuvenate. (ABC News: Toby Hunt)

Asked whether he had threatened to terminate Mr Stevens, Mr Marks denied doing so.

"I don't know where that information is coming from, Senator. Sorry," Mr Marks said.

"I don't make threats, Senator."

Marks confirmed the incoming news director had been chosen through a hiring process run by an external provider.

However, the managing director also noted he had been scouting "informally" for the role over the past six months, knowing Stevens "was potentially leaving".

Marks declined to answer questions from Greens senator Mehreen Faruqi whether Reuters executive Simon Robinson would be the next news director, as reported by the Guardian.

ABC to 'refresh and rejuvenate' under new director

Stevens said yesterday after 19 years at the organisation he was leaving "for reasons both professional and personal".

The outgoing director said there was "no more complex news organisation" in the country, and during his tenure he had sought to "strengthen and defend our journalism without being blind to our stumbles".

Senator Henderson suggested a number of serious mistakes at the ABC had forced the news director's resignation, naming the defamation loss to Heston Russell over allegations against him reported by the ABC in 2021, and a 2022 investigation where additional gunshots were wrongly added to a clip of Australian troops firing from a helicopter in Afghanistan's Helmand province in 2012.

Marks said he would not go into details on Stevens's resignation, but said it provided an opportunity to "refresh and rejuvenate" the ABC.

Senator Henderson said it should be an opportunity for the ABC to address what she described as a "culture of defensiveness".

"When some journalists, not all, but when the journalists involved have made these errors, too often the ABC has backed them in rather than appreciate we have made a mistake and we need to correct that mistake," Senator Henderson said.

Marks argued standards had improved under his leadership.

"Occasionally the ABC does make errors, as all media organisations do, I agree with you," Marks said.

"We defend where it is appropriate, but we acknowledge our errors when we have made them."

Marks said as the ABC looked ahead, it would seek to be more focused on its strengths.

"The ABC has a number of core responsibilities, many things that only we do now, and we carry that weight very seriously," Marks said.

"We are at a time in the organisation where we need to say what has served us well in the past may not serve us in the future.

"We keep asking our people to do more, to occupy more platforms, to be in more places at more times. That pressure of more is something we have got to solve for and be very specific and focused on … doing more often leads to spreading resources too thinly."

The ABC and Reuters have been contacted for comment.

title: ‘You get what you deserve’: Why Haas is not entertaining Blues recall – yet
text:
Advertisement

SportNRLState of Origin ‘You get what you deserve’: Why Haas is not entertaining Blues recall – yet Nick Wright May 28, 2026 — 2:16pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Payne Haas says he’s fully fit and ready for a State of Origin recall, but concedes the thought of helping NSW seal the series is not at the forefront of his mind. Having missed just four potential games for the Blues since his 2019, it was a rare feeling for the Brisbane Broncos enforcer to be watching from the comfort of his couch – admitting to feeling anxious right until the controversial send-off of Queensland fullback Kalyn Ponga in the 57th minute on Wednesday night. Haas had been ruled out of game one selection due to a knee injury in his club’s round seven clash with Wests Tigers, but has been named for his NRL comeback on Sunday against the Dragons at Suncorp Stadium. Payne Haas at Blues training. Getty Images While NSW got the job done and closed out a 22-20 triumph of the Maroons at the death, three of their tries came after Ponga was given his marching orders for what was deemed a shoulder charge by referee Ashley Klein on Tolu Koula. The decision has polarised pundits and fans, with many suggesting the incident was a head clash, despite Ponga failing to wrap his arms as he braced for collision.

Advertisement

Related Article State of Origin The 33 schools and 35 clubs who made Origin heroes – and the man who fell off the radar No Blues forward surpassed 100 running metres on the night, while Newcastle prop Jacob Saifiti was not used. But Haas insists his thoughts are on helping rescue the Broncos out of a three-game losing streak, rather than vying to join his state’s engine room for game two on June 17. “I think I’ll just stay present here and worry about my footy here. Obviously, we’re in a bit of a slump at the moment, so I just want to get back to playing Broncos footy and try to get the win this week,” Haas said. “You get what you deserve. When we get there [to game two selections] we’ll cross that bridge, but obviously playing for your state is always a blessing and it’s a huge honour.

Advertisement

“I was a bit nervous until KP got sent off, after that happened I was pretty confident. I’m just glad the boys got the win; they stuck tough and got the win, so that’s all that matters.” While there was a small hope Haas could have been fit to return for the Broncos’ Magic Round clash with the Warriors, which took place the day before Origin selections, he said he was never going to be ready to perform at his best for NSW. “I knew the extent of my injury and I had to be pretty cautious with it and make sure I’m right for the Broncos first and foremost. That’s my whole focus,” Haas said. Loading Haas makes his Brisbane comeback at a crossroads in their season, and at a time when they are starting to lighten what had become an overflowing casualty ward. Ahead of Magic Round, the Broncos had 13 players sidelined through injuries, suspensions and personal reasons, but Haas will be joined by Kotoni Staggs, Brendan Piakura, Grant Anderson and Jack Gosiewski as players who missed that Warriors’ defeat.

Advertisement

Related Article State of Origin How Sam Walker silenced his critics in controversial Origin debut Haas believes the injury toll – one that now includes edge forward Jordan Riki, who has undergone foot surgery on a plantar fascia issue – was not at fault for the club’s slide, but rather their own ill-discipline had been their downfall. But he was adamant he would be making his own return free of the concerns that hindered his season, and warned he would be back as a more intimidating presence than before. “It’s been a blessing in disguise, to be honest, being injured – just sitting back, observing and having a look at where we can improve as a team and as an individual. I’ve been looking at myself and my own game, and obviously having that has been a blessing,” Haas said. “I’m 100 per cent ready to go, I trained with the team fully the other day, so I’m ready to go, and I’m excited.” Start the day with a summary of the day’s most important and interesting stories, analysis and insights. title: Critical Resources hails sulphur-free battery breakthrough
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Critical Resources hails sulphur-free battery breakthrough Brought to you by Bulls N’ Bears Doug Bright May 28, 2026 — 1:58pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Critical Resources has added weight to its battery technology program after reporting its sulphur-free electrolyte has delivered room-temperature performance comparable to that of leading sulphide-class materials. The company says its first-pass amorphous solid-state electrolyte has returned ionic conductivity of 3.2 milliSiemens per centimetre and activation energy of 0.27 electron volts in testing at the South Dakota School of Mines and Technology in the US. Critical Resources test cells under evaluation on a 48-channel Arbin coin-cell cycler at the South Dakota School of Mines & Technology. Those numbers are two of the key measures used to judge electrolyte performance, with the result indicating lithium ions can move quickly through the material without the temperature sensitivity that has traditionally hampered many rival chemistries. Importantly, the company says the values measured at South Dakota were benchmarked against published, peer-reviewed scientific literature, providing an externally referenced basis for comparisons of its present and future performance.

Advertisement

‘This is early-stage laboratory validation, but the technical merits are very encouraging.’ Critical Resources managing director Tim Wither For Critical, the bigger prize is manufacturability. Sulphide electrolytes are widely regarded as top performers, but they can release toxic gas on contact with moisture and usually need inert-atmosphere processing, which has long been seen as one of the main barriers to commercial scale-up. The company says achieving equal transport performance without sulphur could point to a safer and potentially lower-cost manufacturing route. The result also gives more weight to Critical Resources’ broader battery push. In late April, the company said it had secured an evaluation licence over thermal management technology from Singapore’s Nanyang Technological University before converting it to a full worldwide commercial licence last week. That technology is aimed at cooling lithium-ion batteries and high-density electronics in warmer operating environments by spraying a non-conductive fluid directly onto hot components, giving the company a parallel stream of work around battery heat management.

Advertisement

Critical Resources managing director Tim Wither said: “Benchmarking our first-pass ASE results against published peer-reviewed literature reveals a performance position that is a result of years of combined research by Dr Smirnova and the SDM team. Being competitive on ionic conductivity — and matching sulphide-class activation energy - from an initial composition is a strong starting point.” Critical says its electrolyte work also complements its dry supersonic deposition manufacturing program, with both streams designed to lower materials and manufacturing risk as the company works towards more representative solid-state cells. The company says its new amorphous solid-state electrolyte delivers ionic conductivity competitive with non-sulphide, non-halide amorphous electrolytes and an activation energy comparable to sulphide electrolytes, but without the challenges associated with sulphide-based chemistry. Both test results are from a non-optimised battery electrolyte composition, with further potential improvements likely through ongoing work. Accordingly, the company’s next steps will include improving the electrolyte composition and carrying out interfacial testing to analyse how effectively lithium ions move across the solid-to-solid boundaries. Compression studies will test how well the battery materials stay connected during operation, an important step before the company moves to more advanced battery cells. The work will also focus on making sure the electrolyte works properly with the battery’s cathode before full-cell testing begins.

Advertisement

The company recognises that development is in its early days and it is not yet claiming commercial readiness. However, its latest benchmarked win certainly seems to have given Critical a strong talking point in a crowded battery race. If the laboratory work keeps delivering, the market may start paying closer attention to what is quietly becoming a broader battery play. Is your ASX-listed company doing something interesting? title: print only Gil Scott-Heron review
text:
Now 73 but a bald ball of energy and positivity, the rather under-eulogised half of that team, when not shining melodically with his flute, led the show from his Rhodes piano at centre stage, offered vocal counterpoint in Lady Day and John Coltrane , and in Winter In America provided a liquid jazz vocal in the key of acceptance that “an empire must fall” – something which might have given comfort to NSW Blues players who at that very moment were being smacked mightily at the other end of Sydney. His work holds up.

Gil Scott-Heron’s collaborator Brian Jackson in full flight at the show The Music of Gil Scott-Heron, a tribute to the late poet and musician.

The material is a given, especially in a tight setlist of about a dozen songs from a productive decade beginning in the early ’70s for the late Scott-Heron and his chief collaborator and cowriter, Brian Jackson.

You can see the bones – actually, to be fair, a good bit of the flesh too – of a special show in this tribute to the work of poet/proto-rapper/singer/activist Gil Scott-Heron.

In Home Is Where The Hatred Is, the blend of Stevie Wonder, Norman Whitfield and Latin jazz felt potent; A Prayer For Everybody To Be Free built to a glowing euphoria that overwhelmed the built-in despair; the closing Guerrilla raised the optimism further when realism might have suggested otherwise.

The Music of Gil Scott-Heron, a tribute to Gil Scott-Heron live at the Sydney Opera House. Ravyna Jassani

That’s why it was a shame for what clearly were some quality musicians and for these lithe songs that the sonic imbalance regularly crunched guitar and the two extra flautists under an assault of drums and occasionally over-prominent bass, while the dual keys would merge into a wash.

Also mixing the good and less good was rapper/vocalist Yasiin Bey, whose representation of Scott-Heron is not mimicry or even strictly speaking a tribute. His own charisma was convincing, his smoother delivery often soothing rather than inflammatory. Opening the show with The Revolution Will Not Be Televised, a song that is 50 per cent rhythm and flute and 50 per cent tone, gave Bey a chance to set himself in a comfort zone as he crouched over his microphone, before stretching out with Free Will and reminding everyone that he is more than adequate as a singer.

However, his need to refer to the words occasionally on a stand, or at one point his phone, took us out of any idle thoughts of transubstantiation, and despite his obvious enjoyment it wasn’t until the penultimate song of the set, A Prayer For Everybody To Be Free, as he shed sunnies and cap and declared that “freedom is everybody’s job”, that he really asserted a commanding presence.

For him and the show, there is room to grow into this.

title: Brisbane might be hurting, but top-placed Dockers are taking nothing for granted
text:
Advertisement

NationalWAAFL 2026 Brisbane might be hurting, but top-placed Dockers are taking nothing for granted Justin Chadwick May 28, 2026 — 1:09pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Fremantle coach Justin Longmuir has brushed off Brisbane’s flop against GWS, labelling it an anomaly ahead of Saturday’s clash with the defending premiers at the Gabba. Big questions were raised about Brisbane’s prospects of snaring a third straight AFL flag after they conceded 14 goals in the third quarter of last week’s 78-point loss to GWS. Patrick Voss celebrates a goal during last weekend’s decisive win over St Kilda. AFL Photos In contrast, Fremantle (10-1) are now premiership favourites after putting together a club-record 10-match winning run to move one win clear on top of the table. The Dockers are set to make the trip to Brisbane without star midfielder Hayden Young, who is likely to be sidelined for a third match with concussion.

Advertisement

That’s on top of fellow gun midfielder Caleb Serong still missing with a calf injury. But the big focus on Saturday will be on how Brisbane (6-5) bounce back from the worst third quarter in VFL/AFL history. “They’ve shown this year already why they’re the reigning premiers ... I expect them to be at their best.” Fremantle coach Justin Longmuir on the Brisbane Lions Longmuir isn’t looking too deeply into it, with the seventh-year coach of the firm belief the Lions won’t produce a repeat effort this week. “For me, it’s a bit of an anomaly, a third quarter like that,” Longmuir said on Thursday.

Advertisement

“We just prepare for the trends more than an anomaly like that. Related Article Opinion

AFL 2026 Fremantle face an almighty test at the Gabba. Which player will be key to victory? Paddy Sweeney Nine Sports Presenter “I don’t know whether the weekend makes me any more wary – I’m wary of them anytime. They beat us last year here. “They’ve shown this year already why they’re the reigning premiers. They’ve played some really good footy across the course of the year. I expect them to be at their best.” Longmuir said he was more hopeful than confident Young would play against the Lions, but the odds look slim given the Dockers don’t want to take any risks.

Advertisement

However, the news is better for skipper Alex Pearce, who will return after missing two games with a knee injury. Injury-prone ruckman Sean Darcy will return from a calf injury via the WAFL, with the 27-year-old slated to play about 80 minutes for Peel Thunder. “It’s really important for his fitness build to get through his 80 minutes, knock a bit of rust off his ruck craft,” Longmuir said. “I’m sure everyone’s interested in how many weeks we intend to leave him in the WAFL. “We haven’t really made those decisions. He’s got to build his fitness and build his form, and earn a spot in the team.”

Advertisement

If Darcy can hit some good WAFL form he will challenge Mason Cox for the No.2 ruck role behind in-form big man Luke Jackson, who is in All-Australian form. AAP
